entry.title: Building a CAP App with Fiori Integration – Part 1: Environment & HANA Cloud Setup
entry.content:
In this blog series we are going to see how to create a Basic CAPM Application from scratch through BTP Trial Account and Integrate it with Fiori. 

--> The First Part of this series will explain all the Pre-Requisites to be done before creating CAP Application. 

--> Second Part of this series will explain how to create CAP Application and Integrate it with Fiori.

Cloud Based Applications may look complex from outside but when blocks are perfectly aligned even a beginner can build a powerful CAP application using BAS. Let’s dive in and start arranging those blocks from the ground up.

Tools Required: BAS, HANA Cloud Central

At end of this series we can able to successfully run a CAP Application in Fiori Generated App and able to create entries in HANA Cloud Central based DB from the Fiori App. 

1.  Create a BTP Trial Account/Use existing Global Account for the Development Process. Access to your SubAccount as below from Account Explorer -> SubAccounts. 

2. Now get into Sub Account and Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions as below and then click Create Button. 

3. Under Services tab -> Select SAP HANA Cloud & in Plan select Tools/hana-cloud which makes service compatible for shareable instance. 

Note: If SAP HANA Cloud is not showing under services tab then add it from Entitlements->Add service Plans.

4. After Creation, the SAP HANA Cloud Central tool will be added to subscription as below along with Business Application Studio. Note: Incase if BAS is not added then add that as well from Create Button. 

5. Once the HANA Cloud and BAS set up is done, then navigate to Security->Users and assign these Roles.

Roles Required :

Note: These roles should be assigned to the user and with that HANA Cloud can be accessed.

Before moving into next point lets have a quick overview on what is SAP HANA Cloud Central and BAS. 

SAP HANA Cloud Central - It manages SAP HANA Cloud instances within the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It acts as a centralized dashboard to create, monitor, and maintain both SAP HANA database instances and Data Lake instances.

SAP BAS - SAP Business Application Studio is a cloud-based Integrated Development Environment (IDE) designed specifically to build and extend SAP applications.

Basically SAP HANA Cloud Central helps to manage data through cloud instances and BAS helps in building data. We can decide how the data should look/maintained from BAS.  

Now let's continue with the Pre-Requisites;

6. As a next step, navigate to Cloud Foundry->Spaces and ensure if space dev is available. If that is not there then create dev space using Create space Button. This Space is really important as it acts as a deployment target from CAP where code is deployed and executed.

7. Navigate to HANA Cloud and click on Create Instance. Again, Creation of HANA Cloud Instance is as important as creation of dev space in cloud foundruy. It acts as the database-as-a-service (DBaaS) where application’s data is permanently stored. HANA Cloud instance also contains HDI Containers which act as a bridge between design-time code from BAS into run-time database objects like tables and views.

8. On Type section select Instance configuration as Configure Manually ( Select other options if you want to configure instance in different way ) and Instance type as SAP HANA Database as we are creating Instance for HANA DB. If Data Lake is expected then select Data lake instead of HANA DB. 

9. In section SAP HANA Database General, Cloud foundry tab to be navigated for filling instance related details as the deployment happens for Cloud Foundry. But it is disabled by default. Henceforth user should select Sign in to cloud Foundry Environment and after sign in the Cloud Foundry tab will be enabled. In that tab fill the details as below. 

10. Post filling the important section as above, navigate to other sections where Connection Details and Advanced settings needs to be mentioned. For a Minimalistic App execution these tabs are not required and henceforth I've skipped it. It needs to be filled based on Business expectations. 

11. Enable Data lake if required and click on create instance as below. 

12. Once above process is done, Test Instance will be created for SAP HANA Cloud central as below and it acts as DB for the Developments made from CAP Project. 

13. Now SAP HANA Cloud is all set, Let's Navigate to BAS and create Dev Space. Get back to your subaccount. Navigate Instances and Subscriptions -> Business Application Studio. Click on create Dev space.

14. Define Dev space name and select Full Stack cloud Application option as below. 

15. With that Dev space will be created in BAS. 

From here, let's continue on Part - 2  where full stack CAP Application will be built and Deployed/Integrated with SAP HANA Cloud. 

What we did till now.

-> Created Trial Account

-> Enabled necessary subscriptions and Roles 

-> Set up SAP HANA Cloud Central

-> Created Instance and ensured Dev space is available

-> Set up BAS Dev space. 

PART: 2 Link - Build BTP CAP App with Fiori Integration – Part 2: Implementing End to End App

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-Guided Implementation Workshop Series
entry.content:
SAP HANA Cloud is a fully managed, in-memory cloud database service as part of the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It delivers high-speed processing and real-time analytics, which is especially valuable for data-intensive applications. The platform supports multiple data models, including relational, graph, spatial, JSON, and document store, allowing developers to work with different types of data in one environment. It also includes built-in data virtualization and integration capabilities, enabling organizations to access and combine data from SAP and non-SAP sources.

SAP HANA Cloud provides a comprehensive environment for building intelligent, data-driven solutions. It supports advanced analytics, machine learning, and predictive capabilities directly within the database, which reduces data movement and accelerates insight generation. Developers can use familiar tools and languages such as SQL, Java, Node.js, and Python to create applications and services.
The platform also includes data lake functionality for storing and processing large volumes of less-frequently accessed data at a lower cost, while still allowing seamless querying alongside in-memory data. Its multi-tier storage approach helps organizations balance performance and cost by automatically managing where data resides. Overall, SAP HANA Cloud enables organizations to modernize their data landscape, support real-time decision-making, and drive digital transformation initiatives.

 

🚀 Empower Your Data Journey: Introducing Our New SAP HANA Cloud Expert Guided Implementation Series 

In today’s fast-paced digital landscape, speed of transformation is a must. Businesses across the globe are evolving to make faster, smarter, and more connected decisions by building intelligent, data-driven solutions using advanced analytics, machine learning, and predictive capabilities. To support this evolution, we’re excited to announce our renewed series of Expert-Guided Implementation workshops (EGI) from SAP Enterprise Support designed to help you upskill, modernize, and future-proof your business processes. Whether you're looking to modernize legacy systems, harness the power of cloud data, or unlock real-time insights through analytics, this workshop series offers targeted enablement for every stage of your development. 

*The following EGI sessions will run throughout the year, with quarterly updates to the schedule. 
Each session is limited to 30 individual customer participants.  
**Registration links below direct to SAP Learning content for SAP Enterprise Support, which requires a one-time registration with a valid S-user.

Configuration, Administration, Security and Monitoring topics for SAP HANA Cloud

In this live EGI session we will guide you through the SAP HANA Cloud capabilities as DBaaS, demonstrate the key features available in the latest quarterly release of the tool and show the benefits of using the administration tools for DB Monitoring, security and configuration. For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP HANA Cloud
  • Overview of SAP BTP
  • Introduction and usage of Administration tools
    • SAP HANA Cloud Central
    • SAP HANA Cockpit
    • SAP HANA Database Explorer
  • Managing, Monitoring and Alert Notification in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Workload Management:
    • Managing Tables
    • HANA NSE
  • SAP HANA HDI Overview
  • Introduction and usage of Cloud Foundry CLI 
  • Security Administration and User management

Register Here* 

 

SAP HANA Cloud Modeling

In this live EGI session, we will guide you through the process of modeling in SAP HANA Cloud. After the completion of the session, you will have the knowledge of calculation view usage and design in SAP HANA Cloud utilizing the SAP Business Application studio. Also, we showcase the core capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud for calculation view graphical modelling and provide help on about SQL Script procedures and implementing functions using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Usage of SAP Business Application Studio and Administration tools
  • Deploy SAP Business Application Studio
  • Introduction and modeling Calculation views
  •  Modeling with SAP Business Application Studio and using Database Explorer
  • Modeling features
  • Generating restricted and calculated columns
  • SQL Script usage
  • Performance Analysis, Debug mode & SQL Analyser
  • Overview of Best Practices

 Register Here* 

 

SAP HANA Cloud Multiple Model Programming including Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph

In this live EGI session, we empower customers with the knowledge of SAP HANA Cloud multiple model programming using the latest concepts including vector engine, knowledge graph engine and retrieval augmented generation. After the completion of the workshop, customers will have the knowledge of multiple model engines and how to incorporate artificial intelligence and retrieval augmented generation capabilities.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Understand SAP HANA Cloud architecture and deployment options
  • Introduction to multiple model programming
  • Data Tiering considerations for multiple model programming
  • Overview of SAP Business Application Studio
  • Spatial data concepts in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Understanding spatial features and functions
  • Spatial data modeling and processing
  • Graph processing in SAP HANA Cloud
  • JSON document store in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Automated machine learning (AutoML) in SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP HANA Cloud vector database
  • Capabilities and benefits of multiple model processing with vectors
  • SAP HANA Cloud knowledge graph engine
  • Knowledge graph and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
  • Real-world applications for Retrieval Augmented Generation

Register Here* 

 

Pro-code cloud application programming development through SAP Build and AI (Joule)

In this live EGI session we will empower you with the knowledge of cloud application programming development in SAP HANA Cloud. After the completion of the session, you will have the knowledge of CAP CDS development and design in SAP HANA Cloud and understand how to build and deploying cloud application projects from scratch and with reduced costs utilizing pro-code development using SAP Build Code with Joule. For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda:

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Introduction to SAP Build Code and Prompt Engineering
  • CAP Architecture and SAP Business Application Studio features
  • Build and deploy a CAP project using SAP Business Application Studio
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud using CAP CDS
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud Optimization
  • Building Application using SAP Build Code and Joule
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud Management and Administration

Register Here* 

Learning Outcomes from the EGI Series:

  • Configuration, Administration, Security and Monitoring topics for SAP HANA Cloud:
    Master SAP HANA Cloud configuration, performance monitoring and secure user management using standard administration tools.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Modeling:
    Develop Calculation views and SQL Script procedures within SAP Business Application Studio.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Multiple Model Programming including Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph:
    Implement advanced Vector Engine, Knowledge Graph, and Spatial Data capabilities to power Generative AI and Retrieval Augmented Generation scenarios
  • Pro-code cloud application programming development through SAP Build and AI (Joule):
    Build and deploy end-to-end CAP applications efficiently using SAP Build Code and Joule


Who Should Attend? 

This workshop series is ideal for developers, modelers, business analysts, system administrators, database administrators and SAP customers looking to transform their business processes. Whether you're just getting started or want to go deeper into SAP's modern toolset, there’s a session tailored for your needs. 

 

GET MORE CONTENT LIKE THIS!

 SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for Foundational Success Plan support and empowerment — have a dedicated SAP Community Group.

As part of your SAP cloud subscription, you get access to:

  • Expert-led resources
  • Tailored recommendations
  • Advisory services
  • …all designed to help you accelerate innovation and success.


👉 Visit our Community Group
Sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID, then hit “Subscribe” to stay updated.

Looking to dive deeper into data-driven innovation?
Within the group, explore the Data & Analytics Value Map to connect with your Foundational Support team. Simply click “Join Group”, and once inside, you can even request a call with a topic expert to get personalized guidance and enablement support.

 

 

 

 

 

Quicklinks

  • SAP Business Data Cloud : Expert-Guided Implementation series
  • Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment: The Value of Continuous Quality Checks
  • SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps - Community Group
  • SAP Enterprise Support - Guided Learning

entry.title: From Idea to App in Minutes: Generating SAP Fiori Elements Applications with the Project Accelerator
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori tools Project Accelerator is an AI-assisted capability that transforms business requirements provided as text, image, or a combination of both into a SAP Fiori elements application. The generated application can be backed by either the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) or ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP).

The Project Accelerator automates the generation of data models, services, UI scaffolding, and mock data, enabling you to quickly turn ideas from brainstorming or whiteboarding sessions into tangible applications that can be immediately previewed and iterated upon.

 

Where it fits in the SAP Fiori Tools ecosystem

The Project Accelerator is built directly into SAP Fiori tools and can be used within SAP BAS.

It complements existing SAP Fiori tools capabilities such as project generation, application preview, and annotation editing. By generating initial project artifacts directly from business requirements, the Project Accelerator helps you quickly establish a solid project structure.

 

What the Project Accelerator Generates

Depending on the selected programming model, the Project Accelerator generates the following artifacts:

CAP:

For CAP-based applications, the Project Accelerator generates:

  • An SAP Fiori elements application
  • Data models and services
  • Local mock data

 

RAP :

For RAP-based applications, the Project Accelerator generates:

  • An SAP Fiori elements application
  • A RAP back-end artifact
  • An OData V4 service endpoint
  • Local mock data

 

Important note: Business logic is not automatically generated in either CAP or RAP projects. Developers must implement the application logic manually after the initial scaffolding is created.

 

 

Step-by-step guide for the RAP Accelerator Through Business Application Studio (BAS)

In this guide, we will generate an SAP Fiori elements app for managing sports games using the RAP Accelerator with image input. This app will allow users to view game schedules, team information, and game details. Later, we will modify the application to target soccer.

 

 

 

1) Prepare your input

Before starting the generation process, gather your business requirements, which are the inputs you prove to the Project Accelerator.

The Input can be in the following formats:

 

Text

You can write a clear description of your application, including:

  • The purpose of the app
  • Entities and their relationships
  • Fields and properties
  • Labels and restrictions
  • Desired UI layout

Tip: Field values, logic, and restrictions are usually easier to describe using text.

 

Images

You can upload images to describe the desired structure or layout, such as:

  • A data model diagram
  • A UI mockup
  • A List Report layout showing columns
  • Object Page sections or tables
  • Screenshots of an existing application

Tip: Layouts and visual structures are easier to describe using images.

 

I'll use the following image to build a sports game management scenario. Users will be able to see which teams are playing against each other, the league and age group, and where and when the game takes place. The app will also display team details, including the trainer and players.

 

Figure 1. Database model for sports game management scenario

When I first generated the application without specifying a sport type, the underlying LLM inferred the context and generated soccer-related mock data. To see how changes to the input such as specifying the sport type affects the output, I regenerated the application with several modifications to the original image. The results, shown in the Appendix , demonstrate how the AI adapts the generated data based on more specific input.

 

Using Multimedia Input

It is important to keep in mind that the accelerator accepts a single file as input. If you want to upload both text and images, or multiple images, you should first combine them into a single document, either as a word document file (.docx) or as a markdown (.md) file and later upload the file to the accelerator as the business requirement.

If you are using a .md file, you should also upload the images to your workspace folder.

 

 

2) Set up your environment:

To access the Project Accelerator, you need to be in Dev Space with the necessary extensions installed.

Prerequisites

  1. You need to have access to SAP Build Code (What is SAP Build Code | SAP Help Portal)  or signed in for (SAP Build Code Test Drive | SAP Tutorials)
  2. You need to use SAP Business Application Studio as your integrated development environment

 

Required Extensions

Based on your programming model, ensure your Dev Space includes the appropriate extensions:

(If you are creating a new Dev Space, you can select an application type that includes the extensions you need: creating a new Dev Space.)

 

For CAP:

Dev Space must include:

  • CAP Tools
  • SAP Fiori Tools

 

For RAP

Dev must include:

  • SAP Fiori Tools

 

RAP Backend Requirements:

For RAP projects, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  • RAP backend should be version 2602 or higher
  • You should be connected to the backend system by configuring the destination in SAP Business Application Studio
  • S_DEVELOP authorization

 

Creating a Dev Space

Now, moving on with the creation of a Dev Space:

  1.  Open SAP Build
    1. Click the icon with small squares on the top-right corner
    2. Select Dev Space ManagerFigure 2. How to access the Dev Space Manager
  2. Create a New Dev Space
    • Enter a name for your Dev Space
    • Select the application type 
      • Each application type includes a predefined set of extensions.
      • Select the application type based on the extensions it provides and whether they are required for your programming model or not.
      • For this example, I selected the Full-Stack ABAP Application.

         

    • Click on “Create Dev Space” Figure 3. How to create a Dev Space
  3. After creating your dev space, you will be redirected to the Dev Space Manager, where you can see the newly created dev space and access any other dev spaces you may have created.Figure 4. Dev Space with status "starting"
  4. Wait until the status changes to "Running"
  5. Click on the Dev Space name to start developing

Image 5. Dev Space with status "running"

 

3)  Launching the Project Accelerator

Once you are in your Dev Space, you can launch the Project Accelerator in multiple ways. One of them being:

Using the Template Wizard

  1. Choose “New Project from Template”Figure 6. Step 1 of launching the Project Accelerator
  2. Select “SAP Fiori Generator" and click "Start"Figure 7 . Step 2 of launching the Project Accelerator
  3. On the next screen, click on the “Project Accelerator” to begin 

Figure 8. Step 3 of launching the Project Accelerator

 

 

4) Choose your programming model accelerator

When using the Project Accelerator, you can either select CAP Accelerator or RAP Accelerator tab depending on your programming model.

 

RAP Configuration

For RAP projects, you must:

  1. Specify the Destination:
    1. Select a destination system from the dropdown
    2. Verify your user authentication (user and password for the backend system)
      • OAuth2 authentication can also be configured via the SAP BTP CockpitFigure 9. Accessing the RAP Accelerator
  2. Configure Backend Connection 

 After successful authentication, additional fields will appear:

Here, you should specify your SAPUI5 repository, the package for RAP backend artifact creation, and the transport request if it is required.

Figure 10. Configuring the backend connection

 

 For more information regarding these fields, please refer to: Generating an Application Using an Image | SAP Help Portal

 

 

5)   Enter Your Business Requirements

After choosing your accelerator and specifying your destination, you can upload the input prepared in Step 1 into the business requirements field.

You can either:

  • Upload files to the workspace and then select them using "Choose File" under the business requirements input area
  • Drag and drop documents directly from the file explorer

Hold the Shift key while dragging

Figure 11. Entering the Business Requirements

 

Note: The clearer and more structured your input is, the better the generated result will be.

 

 

6)   Generating the Project

Note: By default, the application is generated in the currently open folder. If you want the project to be created in a different location, you can specify a Project Folder Path before starting the generation.

  1. Click Generate below the input field:
    • The tool will interpret your requirements and generate the project artifacts.Figure 12. Generating the project
    • The interface switches to a generation view displaying progress. Generation may take some time depending on the complexity of your requirementsFigure 13. Generation view

       

    • Once the generation is complete, the Application Information page will appear.Figure 14. Application Information page

       

    • If the project is not in a workspace folder, BAS may display a dialog asking whether to add it after the generation is complete.Figure 15. Adding the project into a workspace

      In this case, you can choose either:

      • “Open Folder”, which creates a new folder for the project, or
      • “Add Project to Workplace”, adding the project to the existing folder. Selecting one of these options adds the project to the workspace and lands you to the Application Information page.

 

 

7)   Preview of the Application

To preview the application:

  1. Select “Preview Application”Figure 16. Previewing the application
  2. Choose “Start Fiori Run” from the context menu
  3. This launches the generated UI.Figure 17. Preview of the application

     

Note: At this stage, the preview only shows the application structure, without any data.

 

Edge Case: Running the Preview from the ABAP Backend

Make sure that you run the preview directly from SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) when previewing the generated application.

If you launch the preview from the ABAP backend instead of BAS, the UI will not render correctly. This is expected behavior as the Project Accelerator stores UI annotations in the local “annotation.xm” file of your Fiori project, not in backend Metadata Extensions. Therefore, the backend cannot render the UI properly without these local annotations.

If the preview is executed from the ABAP backend, the result will appear similar to the following:

Figure 18. Preview of the application when rendered from the ABAP backend example 1

Figure 19. Preview of the application when rendered from the ABAP backend example 2

 

 

8 ) Generate and Preview with Local Mock Data

You can also preview the application with locally generated mock data:

  1. Open the terminal
  2. Enter Ctrl + C to terminate the current process
  3. Select “Preview Application”
  4. Choose “Start-Mock Fiori Run” from the context menuFigure 20. Previewing the application with locally generated mock data
  5. After restarting, you may need to log in again for system authorization.
  6. The UI will now display generated mock data, allowing you to:

Figure 21. Preview of the application with mock dataFigure 22. Preview of the application with mock data - game object page

Figure 23. Preview of the application with mock data - team object page

 

 

9)   Review and Refine

After generation, review the application and refine the configuration as needed. You can adjust field names, labels, page sections, and UI layout using the Page Map:

  1. Click on "Open Page Map"Figure 24. Navigating to Page Map 
  2. Select the pencil icon next to the location you want to modify 
  3. Make your changes.
  4. The preview will automatically refresh and update

 

Example 1: Editing Column Names

Since the business requirement was for general sports, and soccer uses fields (not courts), let's change the column name from "court" to "field."

  • Scenario: Change the field name “Home Court” to “Home Field”
  1. Click on Open Page Map
  2. Click on the pencil icon of “Teams Object Page”Figure 25. Navigating to "Teams" Object Page
  3. Expand the “Table” section
  4. Expand the “Columns” subsection
  5. Click on the “Home Court” field
  6. On the right-hand side (RHS), find the “Label” field and change it to “Home Field”Figure 26. How to change the field name
  7. The preview will automatically refresh and update

Figure 27. Changing the field name in "Team" Object Page

 

Note: Renaming the column in the Teams Object Page only changes how it appears on the Teams Object Page. The “Home Court” column on the Games Object Page won’t update automatically:

Figure 28. Display of unchanged field name in "Game" Object Page

To change it on the Games Object Page as well, you’ll need to rename the column in the Game Object Page. You can do this just by repeating the steps above for Games Object Page.

 

Example 2: Adding a Description to an Object Page

  •  Scenario: Add the description “Game Information” to the Game Object Page.Figure 29. Empty description space in "Game" Object Page
  1. Click on Open Page Map
  2. Select “Games Object Page”Figure 30. Navigating to "Game" Object Page
  3. Click on “Header”.
  4. On the RHS, find Description Type and select “Text” from the dropdown.Figure 31. Navigating to Description Type dropdown

Choosing “Text” allows you to enter custom free text. Alternatively, you can select “Property” to pick from predefined fields or objects.

 In this case, I selected the “Text” option because “Game Information” is not a predefined feature of any existing object in the application.

5. Type “Game Information” into the Description field.

Figure 32. How to add a text to the description space

6. The UI will automatically refresh and update

Figure 33. Preview of "Game" Object Page with description text

 

Example 3: Adding an Icon to an Object Page

  • Scenario: Adding a soccer icon to the Game object page
  1. Click on Open Page Map
  2. Select the “Games Object Page”
  3. Find the icon sectionFigure 34. Navigating to the Icon Explorer
  4. Click the link to the Icon Explorer
  5. Search for “soccer”Image 35. View of the Icon Explorer and searching for the term "soccer"You can also browse the available icons and select the one that fits your goalsFigure 36. Overview of the existing SAPUI5-supported icons
  6. Copy the soccer ball icon’s URL

Figure 37. Copying the soccer ball icon's URL

7. Switch to your Dev Space and paste the URL into the icon fieldFigure 38. Pasting the copied URL into the Icon field8. The UI will refresh automatically and display the iconFigure 39. Preview of the "Game" Object Page with the soccer ball icon

 

Next steps:

After you are satisfied with the UI Layout;

  • For CAP: Add business logic in service handlers; enrich annotations; refine data model.
  • RAP: Implement behavior logic, validation, determinations; adjust annotations; refine UI.

For more information:

  • Official documentation page:

Generating an Application with the Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI | SAP Help Portal

 

Appendix

1)    Specifying the sport type as volleyball


 To see how specifying the sport type affects the generated mock data, I added additional requirements database model I originally used:Figure 40. Updated database model, sport type specified as volleyball

The generated mock data became more volleyball-oriented, as reflected in the “Team” and “Place” names:

Figure 41. Preview with volleyball-specific mock data

I was also interested to see if the number of players would change based on the specified sport type, for example:

  • a volleyball team having at least 6 players
  • a soccer team having 11
  • a basketball team having 5

However, the number of players did not change compared to the original input.

Figure 42. "Team" Object Page displaying players

 If you want to have specific number of instances, you can explicitly include this in your business requirements:Figure 43. Data model diagram with amount of team members specified

Figure 44. "Team" Object Page with 6 players

 

2)    Using Mock UI

For the Mock UI, I uploaded an image intended to represent the list of all games and the corresponding object page:

Figure 45. Mock UI - First attempt

However, the LLM did not recognize this image as one of its supported input types and returned an error:

If you are providing a Mock UI, the input should be clear and focused to avoid such errors.

Figure 46. Error message for unsupported images

In my experience, Mock UIs produce the best results when the input is as simple and clear as possible. For example, the image below worked well:

Figure 47. Mock UI - Second attempt

The Project Accelerator successfully generated the list report and even added “Players” and “Teams” objects to the object page, which was a positive surprise:

Figure 48. Successfully generated application with Mock UIImage 49. Successfully generated application with Mock UI - Object Page

 

3)    Using Multimedia Input: Multiple images

I combined the input material into a single .docx document and uploaded it to the accelerator:Figure 50. The two images, combined in a single document

You can upload the .docx or .md files the same way as you would for images.

The following application was generated:Figure 51. Preview of the application generated with multi-media input

Figure 52. Preview of the application generated with multi-media input - Object Page

 

Mismatch in object fields  

When combining multiple input sources, such as database tables and Mock UI images, it is important that these models are consistent. Inconsistencies between the data model and the UI definition can lead to incorrect or incomplete application generation:

Figure 53. Multi-media input with mismatching fields

Figure 54. Preview of the application generated with the input shown in Figure 53

 The following issue occurred due to such a mismatch:

  • The “Game” entity contains additional fields in the database model that are not represented in the UI.
  • The “Team” entity is missing a role field in the database model that is expected by the UI.

entry.title: Transforming Manual Sales Order Processing into an Intelligent SAP BTP Workflow
entry.content:
Many organizations still rely on manual, email-driven sales order processing. Business users receive customer documents, extract key details, validate information, and create orders in SAP S/4HANA. While manageable at low volumes, this approach becomes time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to scale as document complexity and volume increase.

Reimagining the Process with SAP BTP

This solution transforms the traditional process into an intelligent, end-to-end workflow using:

  • SAP Build Process Automation for orchestration
  • SAP Document AI for document understanding
  • SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) for validation, enrichment, and user interaction

The result is a centralized, application-driven experience that minimizes manual effort while maintaining business control.

Solution Overview

The automated workflow replaces fragmented manual steps with a streamlined process:

  1. Incoming emails are monitored via SAP Build Process Automation
  2. Attachments are sent to SAP Document AI for data extraction
  3. Document AI extracts relevant sales order fields using a custom schema
  4. A CAP application retrieves and processes the extracted data
  5. Additional validation and enrichment is performed (e.g., master data checks, missing fields, amount validation)
  6. Business users review and approve data in a centralized interface before posting to SAP S/4HANA

Architecture Highlights

The solution is built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP):

  • SAP Build Process Automation → event-driven orchestration
  • SAP Document AI → document extraction
  • SAP CAP → business logic, validation, enrichment, UI
  • SAP S/4HANA → system of record
  • Connectivity & Destinations → secure integration via APIs

This architecture enables scalable automation with human-in-the-loop control.

Why This Approach Matters

Document extraction alone is not enough. True automation requires business context, validation, and enrichment.

The CAP layer bridges this gap by:

  • Combining extracted data with SAP master data
  • Applying business rules and validations
  • Providing a controlled user review interface

This ensures accuracy while reducing manual effort.

Key Advantages

  • Reduced manual handoffs
  • Improved validation and enrichment control
  • Enhanced user experience through a centralized interface
  • Seamless integration with SAP S/4HANA
  • Scalable and future-ready architecture

Business Impact

This solution addresses the core challenge of converting unstructured documents into structured SAP transactions.

Customer results include:

  • ~30% increase in order-processing capacity
  • ~90% reduction in manual effort per transaction
  • Improved accuracy and reduced rework
  • Faster month-end and quarter-end close
  • Cleaner audit trail and compliance

Conclusion

By consolidating extraction, validation, enrichment, and user review into a single workflow, organizations can move from manual processing to intelligent automation - improving efficiency while maintaining control.

If you have any further questions, please leave a comment below or send your inquiries to the email CoE_AI_PlanningSpendExec@global.corp.sap. We will be happy to get back to you.

entry.title: SAP CAPM Project Lifecycle: From Development to Fiori Preview
entry.content:
Let's see an end  to end demonstration for creating the CAPM project, entity, service with annotations, create the entry via Post man tool and Preview the Application in Fiori.

Below mentioned detailed points covered in this Blog,

  1. BTP Cockpit and its properties
  2. Creation of CAP Project
  3. Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central
  4. Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation
  5. Preview in the Fiori Application

BTP Cockpit and its properties:

Create a BTP Cock PIT Trial account and Sub Account.

In the Instances and Subscription Add SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Application Studio.

Create a Space in the Cloud Foundry, which is a shared, isolated location with an organization (org) used to develop, deploy and manage application and services.

In SAP BTP Cockpit administrators navigate to security->Role collections, create or edit a collection, and add roles-derived from application templates-to define the granular access.

Creation of CAP Project:

In this Business Application studio create a Dev space and create a Project as mentioned below.

 

 

 

Once Project created a project with workspace created. Here I have created ENTITY_CRUD project. As per below image under DB Node we can create the entity and service for the entity create under SRV node.

gen

Auto-generated folder created during build; contains compiled artifacts from your CDS models. You normally don’t edit this manually.

 .vscode

Holds VS Code-specific settings like launch configs and workspace preferences for development.

app

Used for UI/front-end apps (like SAP Fiori); contains HTML, UI5, or other frontend resources.

db

Defines your data model and database artifacts using CDS files (like itemrecordsdb.cds) and deployment configs (undeploy.json).

gen

Another generated folder used during build/deploy; stores intermediate files created by CAP tools.

mta_archives

Contains packaged .mtar files used for deployment to SAP BTP (multi-target application archives).

node_modules

Stores all installed npm dependencies required for your CAP project.

srv

Defines the service layer (business logic & APIs); CDS files like itemrecordssrv.cds expose entities as OData services.

.cdsrc-private.json

Local configuration file for CAP (like profiles, credentials); usually not committed to Git.

.gitignore

Specifies files/folders (like node_modules) that Git should ignore.

 eslint.config.mjs

Configuration for code linting to maintain code quality and standards.

mta.yaml

Defines how the application is structured and deployed in SAP BTP (modules, resources, dependencies).

package.json

Main project config file listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

package-lock.json

Locks exact versions of installed dependencies for consistent builds.

 README.md

Documentation file describing the project, setup, and usage.

An entity with Itemrecords created. Here I have used ‘manage, cuid’   from @sap/cds/manage which will create a UUID key for every record creation.

Create a service for itemrecords db which acts as an interface between your data models and outside the world like UI apps or external systems.

Once entity and service generated in the project deploy the project using cf deploy mta_archives/<file>.mtar bash commands. After successful deployment we can see service instances created in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

To link the created entity in BAS to SAP HANA Cloud create a service key for the <entity>-db instance as shown from the below steps.

Choose Entity_CRUD-db and click on the navigation panel where service key need to create. In this illustration I have created as ENTITY_CRUD-db-key.

Once service key generated, we can see the JSON file in the view parameter. This JSON file needs to upload in the SAP HANA explorer to link the DB.

Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central:

Navigate to Subscribed SAP HANA CLOUD and create instances which allows you to use in-memory computing for real-time transactional and analytical processing. Once instance is created it should be in Running status which indicates Active, available for SQL connections and able to perform data processing, backups and administrative tasks.

Now, open the SAP HANA database Explorer in the SAP HANA cloud central, click on the + icon where instance type to be chosen as HDI container and choose Apply from JSON string where need to paste the JSON file from the service key entity in the BTP cockpit.

 

 

 

 

Once JSON file applied Entity from the BAS linked to the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 

 

Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation:

To Authenticate authorization in the Post Man tool go to <entity>-auth in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

 

 

Here I have navigated to the ENTITY_CRUD-auth where service key SK-ENTITY_CRUD is created once service key created a JSON file generated with client ID, client secret, URL.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Client ID, URL client secret mapped to the Authorization in the post man tool and generate the Access Token.

 

 

 

 

Using post operation can create a data to the entity in the CAP project.

 

 

 

Successful created entries showing in the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 Preview in the Fiori Application:

To Preview the data in the Fiori application, In the CAP project search with Open Application Generator and create a List Report page.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Once Application created, In the project terminal using Cds watch –profile hybrid can view the preview of the Application in Fiori

Required Annotations can use in the srv which will be reflect in the Fiori Application.

 

Conclusion
This demonstration showcased the seamless integration between SAP Business Application Studio, SAP HANA Cloud, and the Fiori front-end. By leveraging annotations and the CAP service layer, we successfully created a functional CRUD application with automated UUID management and secure API access. You are now ready to extend this project with custom business logic or complex UI enhancements.

entry.title: Build Solutions for SAP Cloud ERP with confidence
entry.content:
Partner Reference Application Family provides Partners with a “golden path” to becoming a SaaS provider of multi-tenant applications based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). The guidance covers building, running, and integrating scalable full-stack cloud applications. Whether you are building a brand-new SaaS product or upgrading an existing one, these resources act as a blueprint for success.

It provides ready-to-run code branches and tutorials to accelerate the development of scalable SaaS solutions. It consists of multiple public SAP-samples repositories on GitHub, offering comprehensive guidance aligned with the “principle of one” and “clean core”. These resources highlight the value of the extensibility environment, demonstrate best practices in code quality and architecture, and serve as demo-ready examples for realistic partner extensions.

CAP Partner Reference Application
A GitHub repository featuring the sample multi-tenant solution “Poetry Slam Manager” built on SAP BTP CAP, along with detailed tutorials for developers and application managers.

When delivering standardized SaaS solutions to multiple customers, customer-specific requirements are inevitable. Using SAP Build and CAP, the solution is extensible out of the box, enabling easy customization. The companion repository, "Partner Reference Application Extension", provides guidance on building and deploying such extensions, including a sample customer-specific extension (“Catering Management”).

ABAP Partner Reference Application

For building ABAP-based side-by-side multi-tenant applications for SAP Cloud ERP, refer to the end-to-end guide. It includes the sample solution “Music Festival Manager” based on ABAP Cloud.

Cross Stack Partner Reference Application
Designed for scenarios requiring on-stack extensions on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with seamless integration to SAP BTP CAP applications.
This example demonstrates how an on-stack extension integrates with a side-by-side CAP multi-tenant application (Poetry Slam Manager). It includes tutorials to help build and integrate both components, enabling enterprise-grade cross-stack extensions using ABAP Cloud and CAP.

On-Stack Partner Reference Application
Focused on delivering on-stack extensions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with scalability, upgrade stability, and seamless integration.
This application integrates with Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 (SAP Fiori app) and showcases event-driven loyalty program management. It demonstrates how standard SAP business processes can be enhanced without modifying the core. Top of Form

 

The Partner Reference Application Family is your accelerator for building next-generation SAP solutions. It’s not just about code—it’s about enabling partners to deliver scalable, extensible, and future-proof applications with speed and quality. By leveraging these ready-made blueprints and best practices, you can reduce development effort, ensure clean core compliance, and focus on what truly matters: delivering value to your customers.

entry.title: Vibe Coding : Unlocking the Power of AI‑Assisted Development with VS Code, Cline, and SAP AI Core
entry.content:
AI‑assisted development is transforming how developers build, test, and optimize applications. Gone are the days of switching endlessly between documentation, terminals, and dashboards. Today, intelligent coding tools like Cline, integrated directly into VS Code, work alongside developers — understanding context, planning steps, and even writing code interactively.
When connected with SAP AI Core, this experience is elevated to an enterprise‑grade level: you can harness SAP’s generative AI capabilities right inside your Node.js projects without leaving your IDE. In this blog, we’ll walk through a complete setup and workflow to unleash this power step by step.   
 
🧩 Step 1: Set Up Your Prerequisites
Before we dive in, make sure you have:
  • VS Code (latest version)
  • Node.js 18+ installed
  • SAP BTP account with AI Core access (extended plan recommended)
  • Cline extension for VS Code
🧩 Step 2 : Installing Cline
  • Open VS Code.
  • Go to the Extensions tab (Ctrl+Shift+X or Cmd+Shift+X).
  • Search for “Cline”.
  • Install the official extension (no additional fork needed).
  • Restart VS Code.

 
🧩 Step 3 : Connect Cline to SAP AI Core
SAP AI Core gives Cline access to enterprise‑grade generative models in your SAP BTP environment. Integration is simple.
Get Your Service Key
  1. In your SAP BTP Cockpit, go to your subaccount.
  2. Under Services → Service Marketplace, find SAP AI Core.
  3. Create an instance (plan: extended).
  4. Generate a Service Key and copy the following information:
    • Client ID
    • Client Secret
    • Auth URL
    • Base URL
    • Resource Group (usually "default")
Please refer the steps as mentioned https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-core-genaihub-provisioning..html=
Step 4: Configure Cline In VS Code

 

In VS Code, open Cline → Settings → API Configuration, and fill in:

 
Cline Setting SAP AI Core Value
AI Core Client Idclientid
AI Core Client Secretclientsecret
AI Core Base URLserviceurls/AI_API_URL
AI Core Auth URLauth_url
AI Core Resource Group"default"

Once saved, Cline will connect securely to your SAP AI Core environment. You're now running on SAP’s Generative AI Hub infrastructure.

 

Step 5: Verify That Cline Is Working
  1. Click the Cline icon in the VS Code sidebar
  2. Type a test prompt, such as: “Hi”
  3. Press Enter
💡Step 6: Start a Node.js Project with AI

Now it’s time to let Cline assist you from the beginning.

 Project Setup Instruction
## Step 1: Create a  project 
Create an empty Nodejs CAP project 
this creates 
 

 

## Step 2: Environment Setup
Create a environment  file and set the port to 4000
## Step 3: Express Server Setup
Create a file to load the env variables, and start express on that port. Add a simple /health route and add a GET /employees route that uses axios to fetch users from "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users" and return only their name and email

## Step 4: CAP Data Model

 

Create a entity called Employees that has ID as Integer, name as String, and email as String
## Step 5: CAP Service Definition
Create a service to expose the Employees entity as a projection, and add an unbound action called fetchTodos that takes an employeeId Integer and returns a String array
this creates
## Step 6: Override Employees READ Operation

 

Override the READ of Employees. Do NOT use a database. Instead, use axios to GET https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users, map the results to match the CDS entity fields, and return the array
 ## Step 7: Implement fetchTodos Action

 

Implement the fetchTodos action in the same file. Use axios to get todos from https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/:employeeId/todos and return an array of just the todo titles

## Step 8: Run Locally

You can either use Cline to run and scaffold the project, or directly execute the standard CAP command like cds watch to start the application and test the services.

🎯Conclusion

Combining VS Code, Cline, and SAP AI Core brings a new era of Node.js development — one where natural language drives coding, SAP‑grade AI enriches solutions, and productivity soars. From scaffolding APIs to integrating intelligent services, this workflow empowers developers to focus on creativity instead of configuration.

The fusion of agentic coding and SAP’s enterprise AI stack isn’t just productivity‑boosting — it’s redefining how SAP developers build the future.

Ready to start vibing?
💬Install Cline, connect to your SAP AI Core, and experience AI‑first Node.js development today.

entry.title: How We Built ASTRA Joule Agent for Automation Testing Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

In my experience working on multiple SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementations, one phase that consistently stands out as challenging is testing. No matter how well the system is designed, testing often becomes time-consuming and difficult to manage—especially when dealing with multiple modules like Employee Central, Payroll, and integrations.

During several projects, I observed that teams were spending a significant amount of effort on repetitive manual validation, coordination across systems, and analyzing failures. While automation tools helped to some extent, they still required heavy setup and maintenance.

This led us to explore how we could make testing more efficient and intelligent using SAP BTP and AI capabilities. That’s how we started working on what we now call the ASTRA Joule HR Agent—a solution aimed at simplifying and improving the testing experience.

The Challenge We Faced

From a practical standpoint, testing in SAP SuccessFactors is not just about executing scripts—it involves validating complete business processes and ensuring data consistency across systems.

Some of the common challenges we encountered were:

  • Handling large regression cycles with hundreds of scenarios
  • Managing complex and realistic test data
  • Difficulty in identifying the root cause of failures
  • Constant switching between multiple tools (SuccessFactors, test tools, Jira, etc.)

These issues often slowed down release cycles and required significant manual effort.

Our Approach – ASTRA Joule HR Agent

To address these gaps, we built ASTRA as a centralized, intelligent powerhouse on SAP Business Technology Platform. We leveraged SAP Business AI through Joule skills to create a conversational, "human-centric" testing experience.

Instead of navigating complex menus, users can interact with ASTRA through SAP Build Work Zone. By integrating Joule, we’ve made it possible to manage tests, gain insights into failures, and even trigger executions using natural language.

Key Technical Highlights:

  • Prebuilt Library: We launched with 926+ automation scripts aligned with SAP Best Practices.

  • Full-Stack Integration: Built using the SAP Cloud Application Programming  model, ensuring seamless OData connectivity with SuccessFactors and S/4HANA.

  • AI Orchestration: Using Joule to provide smarter interactions and faster insights into test logs.

Benefits

1. Extensive Module Coverage

ASTRA doesn’t just "test"; it validates the entire HCM lifecycle. This includes Employee Central, Recruiting, Onboarding, Benefits, Time Management, and Performance & Goals. It even handles custom Extension Center objects, ensuring your unique business logic is never left unchecked.

 

 

2. End-to-End & Integration Testing - Enables both functional and integration testing across SAP SuccessFactors, SAP ERP, SAP S/4HANA, and external systems—helping ensure full end-to-end process validation.

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. AI-Powered Insights (Joule Skills)

By utilizing SAP Business AI, ASTRA helps users understand why a test failed without digging through raw logs. Joule assists in identifying failed scenarios and provides intelligent summaries, drastically reducing investigation time.

 

 

 

 

4. Dynamic, Input-Driven Execution

Efficiency is key. ASTRA allows users to enter specific Entity IDs to run focused tests for individual records. The system dynamically filters and executes only the relevant test cases based on your input data.

 

 

 

5. Automated Documentation & Defect Management

  • Traceability: Every execution generates a PDF-based result document for step-by-step audit trails.

  • Jira Integration: Failed scenarios automatically trigger ticket creation in JIRA, ensuring the engineering team is notified in real-time.

 

 

 

6. Joule-Powered Assistance with SAP Business AI (Joule Skills)

  • Uses SAP Business AI through Joule skills to enhance test execution and analysis.
  • Provides intelligent assistance to help users understand test results and failures faster.
  • Helps identify failed scenarios and related logs without manual analysis.
  • Improves overall testing efficiency by reducing time spent on investigation and coordination.

 

 

Business Value

From what I’ve observed while working with this approach, the impact is quite practical and noticeable in day-to-day project execution:

  • Faster Release Cycles: With better visibility into test results and fewer manual dependencies, we were able to move through release cycles with more confidence and less delay.
  • Reduced Manual Effort: A significant portion of repetitive validation was minimized, which allowed both functional and technical teams to spend more time on critical tasks rather than regression testing.
  • Improved Data Reliability: By validating end-to-end scenarios with realistic test data, we could identify issues earlier and avoid unexpected problems during go-live.
  • Better Visibility for Stakeholders: Having centralized dashboards within SAP Build Work Zone made it easier for stakeholders to track progress, understand results, and make informed decisions without relying on multiple tools.

Conclusion

Based on my experience, testing in SAP SuccessFactors projects often becomes a coordination-heavy activity. What worked well for us with this approach was shifting the focus from managing test execution to actually improving system quality.

By leveraging capabilities available on SAP Business Technology Platform along with Joule, we were able to make testing more streamlined and easier to manage.

The ASTRA Joule HR Agent, as we implemented it, is not just about automation—it’s about making testing more meaningful, reducing effort, and helping teams deliver stable and reliable HR solutions more consistently.

entry.title: Upgrading your SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor: Beyond TinyMCE version 4
entry.content:
Rich Text Editor in SAPUI5

The Rich Text Editor is an SAPUI5 control for data input and editing. It is used to enter rich text with different styles, colors, and formatting. The Rich Text Editor allows users to format text and add different types of elements, such as images and hyperlinks.

Screenshot of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor control

See details on

  • sap.ui.richtexteditor.RichTextEditor: Design, documentation, API reference and samples
  • RichTextEditor building block: Documentation and documentation in SAP Fiori Development Portal

Dependency on TinyMCE

For its implementation, this control uses the third-party component TinyMCE (see https://www.tiny.cloud/). When Tiny deprecates a version of TinyMCE, this version might still be in use in some versions of SAPUI5. Should issues arise, the SAPUI5 team tries to fix them by working around gaps. However, sometimes this is not possible, and updating TinyMCE is necessary, even in maintenance versions of SAPUI5. This is especially important for security, an area where SAPUI5 must address upcoming risks for all customers.

This dependency adds restrictions to the usage and support of the Rich Text Editor control, see the documentation. If your app uses a deprecated version of TinyMCE, the console shows a respective error message stating that this version of TinyMCE is deprecated and will be removed in future.

What happens in July 2026?

 

SAPUI5 will stop loading the deprecated TinyMCE version 4 by default.
Instead, it will load the latest available version.

This will happen in the following SAPUI5 versions:

  • SAPUI5 1.71   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.71.62
  • SAPUI5 1.84   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.84.42
  • SAPUI5 1.96   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.96.28
  • SAPUI5 1.108 - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.108.0 

What does this mean for you?

As always, you benefit from improved features and qualities of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor. If, and only if, your app relies on TinyMCE version 4, the app will break until you use the EditorType property to explicitly stay with version 4. SAP does not recommend using deprecated software, as explained above.

What is planned for 2026?

The SAPUI5 team further plans to permanently remove TinyMCE version 4 from the SAPUI5 code later in 2026. This means there will be no way to load it anymore, no will workaround available. SAP strongly recommends removing any dependency to TinyMCE version 4.

Call to Action

Avoid any dependency on a specific (deprecated) version of TinyMCE to always benefit from the latest features and qualities in your SAPUI5 apps.

entry.title: How to Restrict CRUD Operations in SAP CAPM Using @Capabilities, @readonly and Test APIs with .http
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, 

Today, I would like to share a blog about restricting CRUD operations in SAP CAPM using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotations and testing APIs with .http files. 

In SAP CAPM, entities are automatically exposed as OData services with CRUD operations. However, in many business scenarios, developers need to control which operations users are allowed to perform on specific entities. 

Using '@Capabilities', we can control Insert, Update, and Delete operations individually for entities. Using '@readonly', we can make an entity completely read-only by allowing only GET operations. These annotations help protect business data from unwanted modifications and improve API security. 

Below, I’ll guide you through a simple step-by-step process to implement CRUD restrictions using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly', and test APIs directly inside BAS using .http files. 

Overview 

CRUD stands for: 

Create → POST  
Read → GET  
Update → PATCH / PUT  
Delete → DELETE  
By default, SAP CAPM allows all CRUD operations for entities exposed through services. 

In real-world applications, this can create issues such as: 

Users accidentally deleting records  
Unauthorized updates to important data  
Business rules requiring read-only entities  
To solve this, SAP CAPM provides the '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotation. 

Using this annotation, developers can: 

Allow or restrict insert operations  
Allow or restrict update operations  
Allow or restrict delete operations  
Make entities completely read-only using '@readonly'  
Protect business 
For demonstration purposes, I am using an employee entity, for example. 

Step 1: Create Employee Entity 


Open the following file: 

db/datamodel.cds 

Add the following Employee entity: 

namespace dip.db; 
using { cuid , Currency} from '@sap/cds/common'; 
using { dip.common } from './common'; 

type Guid : String(32); 

context master { 
entity employee : cuid
{ 
    nameFirst: String(40); 
    nameMiddle: String(40); 
    nameLast: String(40); 
    nameInitials: String(40); 
    sex : common.Gender; 
    language: String(5); 
    phoneNumber: common.PhoneNumber; 
    email: common.Email; 
    loginName: String(20); 
    Currency: Currency; 
    salaryAmount: common.AmountT; 
    accountNumber: String(16); 
    bankId: String(8); 
    bankName: String(64); 
    validFrom: Date; 
    validTo: Date; 
} 
}
 
 Step 2: Expose Entity in Service Layer 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Add the following code: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 
service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService')  

{    
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee;
}
 
Step 3: Create Common Types File 

Before exposing the entity in the service layer, we will create a reusable common file for shared data types and validations. 

In SAP CAPM, common.cds is used to define reusable: 

  • custom types  
  • validations  
  • enums  
  • aspects  

This helps reduce duplicate code and improves project maintainability. 

Open the following file:

db/common.cds

Add the following code: 

namespace dip.common; 
using { } from '@sap/cds/common'; 

type Gender : String(20) enum 
{ 
  Male          = 'M'; 
  Female        = 'F'; 
  nonBinary     = 'N'; 
  noDisclosure  = 'D'; 
  selfDescribe  = 'S'; 
}; 

type AmountT : Decimal(15, 2) @( 
  Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'CURRENCY_CODE', 
  sap.units                     : 'CURRENCY_CODE' 
); 

aspect Amount { 
  CURRENCY_CODE : String(4); 
  GROSS_AMOUNT  : AmountT; 
  NET_AMOUNT    : AmountT; 
  TAX_AMOUNT    : AmountT; 
} 

type PhoneNumber : String(14) 
  @assert.format.pattern : '((?:\\+|00)[17](?: |\\-)?|(?:\\+|00)[1-9]\\d{0,2}(?: |\\-)?|(?:\\+|00)1\\-\\d{3}(?: |\\-)?)(0\\d|([0-9]{3})|[1-9]{0,3})(?:((?: |\\-)[0-9]{2}){4}|((?:[0-9]{2}){4})|((?: |\\-)[0-9]{3}(?: |\\-)[0-9]{4})|([0-9]{7}))'; 

type Email : String(255) 
  @assert.format.pattern : '^([a-zA-Z0-9_\\-\\.]+)@([a-zA-Z0-9_\\-\\.]+)\\.([a-zA-Z]{2,5})$';
 
Step 4: Run the CAPM Application 

After creating the entity, service layer, and common types file, the next step is to run the SAP CAPM application. 

Open the terminal in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) and execute the following command: 

cds watch

The cds watch command starts the CAPM server and automatically reloads the application whenever changes are made in CDS files or service files. 

Once the application starts successfully, you will see the server running on: 

http://localhost:4004 

You can now access the OData services from the browser. 

 

Step 5: Open OData Service

Open the following URL in browser: 

localhost:4004/CatalogService/ 
You will see the available entity sets including: 

EmployeeSet 

 
Step 6: Create. http File for API Testing 

In SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), create a new file named: 

test.http 

The .http file is used to test APIs directly inside BAS without external tools like Postman. 

Add the following requests inside test.http: 

### employeeSet top 5 
GET http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet?$top=5 

### INSERT Employee 
POST http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet 
Content-Type: application/json 
{ 
  "nameFirst": "Dip", 
  "nameMiddle": "Ramesh", 
  "nameLast": "Ragho", 
  "nameInitials": "DRR", 
  "sex": "F", 
  "language": "en", 
  "phoneNumber": "9876500001", 
  "email": "raj1@gmail.com", 
  "loginName": "rajkumar1", 
  "Currency_code": "INR", 
  "salaryAmount": 56000, 
  "accountNumber": "123456789001", 
  "bankId": "CBI001", 
  "bankName": "Central Bank", 
  "validFrom": "2024-01-01", 
  "validTo": "2030-12-31" 
} 

### UPDATE Employee 
PATCH http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet('111e8400-e29b-41d4-a716-446655440001') 
Content-Type: application/json   

{ 
  "salaryAmount": 56000 
} 

### DELETE Employee 
DELETE http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet('111e8400-e29b-41d4-a716-446655440001')

Using .http files, developers can: 

  • test CRUD operations  
  • verify API responses  
  • test restrictions from '@Capabilities'  
  • test read-only behavior from '@readonly'

directly inside BAS. 

Run the POST request from the .http file to insert employee data. 

 
Step 7: Apply CRUD Restrictions Using '@readonly' and '@Capabilities'

After successfully testing the APIs, the next step is to apply CRUD restrictions on the EmployeeSet entity using '@readonly' and '@Capabilities' annotations. 

These annotations help control which operations users are allowed to perform on the entity. 

Using '@readonly'

The '@readonly' annotation makes the entity completely read-only. 

Only the GET operation is allowed. 
POST, PATCH, and DELETE operations are automatically restricted. 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Update the code as shown below: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 
service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService') 

{  
    @readonly 
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee; 
}

Testing '@readonly'

Now run the same API requests from the .http file. 

You will notice: 

  • GET request works successfully  
  • POST request fails  
  • PATCH request fails  
  • DELETE request fails  

Expected error response: 

405 Method Not Allowed 

Expected Output 

Below you can see the screenshots of the API responses generated during testing. 

successful GET response  

 

 failed POST response

 

failed PATCH response

 

failed DELETE response 

 
 Using '@Capabilities'

The '@Capabilities' annotation provides more flexibility by allowing developers to control Insert, Update, and Delete operations individually. 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Update the code as shown below: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 

service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService') {  

   @Capabilities : { 
   Insertable  : true, 
   Updatable   : false, 
   Deletable   : false 
} 
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee; 
}

Allowed and Restricted Operations 

Operation 

Status 

GET 

Allowed 

POST 

Allowed 

PATCH 

Restricted 

DELETE 

Restricted 

Testing '@Capabilities' 

Run the API requests again using the .http file. 

You will notice: 

  • GET request works  
  • POST request works  
  • PATCH request fails  
  • DELETE request fails  

Expected restricted response: 

405 Method Not Allowed 

Expected Output 

Below you can see the screenshots of the API responses generated during testing. 

GET success response  

 

POST success response  

 

PATCH restricted response

 

DELETE restricted response 

 

Conclusion 

In this blog, we learned how to restrict CRUD operations in SAP CAPM using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotations. We also explored how to test APIs directly inside SAP Business Application Studio using .http files. 

These annotations help developers secure APIs, control user operations, and protect business data from unwanted modifications. 

Thank you for reading this blog. I hope this blog helps you understand CRUD restriction concepts in SAP CAPM more effectively.

 

Thanks and Regards, 

Deepa Ramesh Ragho

entry.title: How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Introduction

If you've built a custom Fiori application on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) and want to expose it as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone, this tutorial walks you through the complete process — from deployment in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to the final working tile in Work Zone.
This guide assumes you already have:
  • A working RAP-based OData service on S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • A Fiori application project in BAS that consumes this service
  • Access to SAP Build Work Zone (standard edition)
  • The app accessible via ADT preview

By the end of this tutorial, your app will be accessible as a tile in your Work Zone site, sitting alongside other tiles like My Inbox.

Architecture Overview

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)
        │
        │  Deploy to ABAP Repository
        ▼
SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 
   ├── OData Service (Service Binding)
   ├── IAM App (External App) + LADI
   ├── Business Catalog
   └── Business Role
        │
        │  Semantic Object + Action
        ▼
SAP Build Work Zone (BTP)
   └── Tile → resolves intent → opens S/4 app via SSO

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system with developer access
  • SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with a destination configured to your S/4 system
  • SAP Build Work Zone subscription on your BTP subaccount
  • A working Fiori app project in BAS (currently accessible via ADT preview only)
  • Both BTP and S/4 trusting the same SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) tenant for SSO

Step 1: Configure crossNavigation in manifest.json

Before deploying, your Fiori app needs to declare how it will be launched from the Fiori Launchpad. This is done via the crossNavigation/inbounds section in manifest.json.
Open webapp/manifest.json and add the following inside the "sap.app" section:
"crossNavigation": {
  "inbounds": {
    "intent1": {
      "semanticObject": "ZObjectName",
      "action": "display",
      "signature": {
        "parameters": {},
        "additionalParameters": "allowed"
      },
      "title": "App Title",
      "subTitle": "SAP Fiori application",
      "icon": "sap-icon://icon_name"
    }
  }
}
Key points:
  • The semanticObject and action together form the intent (#ZObjectName-display) that the Fiori Launchpad and Work Zone will use to navigate to your app.
  • Choose a meaningful semantic object name (prefix with Z for custom).
  • The title here will appear on the tile.
  • To browse available icons for your tile, see the Icon Explorer . 
Important: Also ensure your i18n property in sap.app uses the simple string format:
"i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
Do NOT use the object format: { "bundleName": "..." }

Note: The S/4HANA Cloud LADI converter cannot process the object format for i18n. Use the simple string format instead. For more details on this issue, see:
UI5 CLI - GitHub Issue #432 
SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help 

Your manifest.json should look like: 

 

Step 2: Ensure i18n.properties File Exists

Make sure the file webapp/i18n/i18n.properties exists and contains at minimum:
appTitle=App Title
appDescription=SAP Fiori Application
Without this file, the LADI creation will fail with the error: "File is not a 'properties' file for texts."

Step 3: Configure Deployment to ABAP

In BAS, configure the deployment target. There are two ways to do this:
Option A: Via Application Info (UI-based)
  1. Right-click your project and select "Open Application Info"
  2. Click the "Deploy" option
  3. Fill in the deployment configuration fields
Option B: Via terminal command
npm run deploy-config
This launches the same configuration wizard in the terminal.
 
Both approaches will prompt you to fill in the following fields:

FieldValue
Target TypeABAP
DestinationYour S/4 destination 
SAPUI5 ABAP RepositoryA unique name, max 15 chars (eg: ZREPO_NAME)
Deployment DescriptionAny suitable description 
PackageYour ABAP package
Transport RequestYour transport request (or leave empty for $TMP)

This generates a ui5-deploy.yaml file:

Step 4: Deploy the Application

Run the deployment from the BAS terminal:
npm run deploy
This builds the app and deploys it to the S/4HANA Cloud ABAP repository.

What to look for in the output:

SAPUI5 Application has been uploaded and registered successfully
Updated LADIs for UI5 Repository "", package "", transport ""
Inbound intent1 (ZObjectName-display) was converted to LADI ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
Launchpad App Descriptor Item ZREPO_NAME_UI5R was updated
Deployment Successful.

For example:

 

If you see warnings like:
  • "No LADI was created as no inbound is defined in manifest.json" → You're missing the crossNavigation block
  • "Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s) / File is not a 'properties' file for texts" → Change i18n to the simple string format

Step 5: Create an IAM App (External App) in S/4

After deployment, the LADI exists on the system but isn't yet linked to any access control. You need to create a new IAM App to hold it and grant access to the OData service.
  1. Open your S/4HANA Cloud system
  2. Search for and open the "Create IAM App" application
  3. Create a new IAM App with:
    • App Type: External App
    • IAM App ID: A meaningful name 
  4. In the "Services" section:
    • Add your OData service
    • This grants the app authorization to call the backend service
  5. In the "Fiori Launchpad App Descr Items" section:
    • Add the LADI ID: ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
    • This enables Launchpad intent navigation
  6. Save and publish

 

 

Why a separate IAM App?
Your OData service already has its own IAM App (type: Custom Inbound Service App) for API access. The External App IAM App is specifically for Launchpad navigation.

Step 6: Add IAM App to a Business Catalog

  • Open Maintain Business Catalogs (or create a new catalog in ADT)
  • Open or create your catalog 
  • Add your new External App IAM App to this catalog
  • Publish the catalog

 

 

Step 7: Assign Business Catalog to a Business Role

  • Open Maintain Business Roles
  • Open the Business Role assigned to your users
  • Go to Assigned Business Catalogs
  • Add your catalog 
  • Save

Step 8: Verify on S/4 Fiori Launchpad

Before adding the tile in Work Zone, verify the app works natively on S/4:
https://<your-system>.lab.s4hana.cloud.sap/ui#ZObjectName-display
Your app should open without errors.
If you get "navigation target could not be resolved", double-check:
  • The LADI is added to the IAM App
  • The IAM App is in a Business Catalog
  • The Business Catalog is in your user's Business Role
  • You've logged out and back in after making changes

Step 9: Add the Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Now that the app works on S/4 via intent navigation, add it to Work Zone:
  • Open SAP Build Work Zone administration
  • Go to Content Manager
  • Create a new App
In the Navigation tab:
  • Semantic Object: ZObjectName (same as the one in manifest.json)
  • Action: display
In the Visualization tab:
  • Title, subtitle, icon as desired
  • Save the app

Step 10: Assign to Role and Group

  • In Content Manager → Roles: Open your role and assign the new app
  • In Content Manager → Groups: Open your group (e.g., "SBPA") and add the app
  • Ensure the role is mapped to a Role Collection that your users have

Step 11: Verify the Tile in Work Zone

Open your Work Zone site.
Your new tile should appear in the designated group/section.
Click it — the app opens seamlessly via SSO (since both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant).

Troubleshooting

Issue CauseFix
"No LADI was created" during deployMissing crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.jsonAdd the crossNavigation block and redeploy
"Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s)"i18n uses object formatChange to "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
"Navigation target could not be resolved"LADI not linked to IAM App, or missing catalog/role assignmentAdd LADI to External App IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role
403 Forbidden / UCON blockedTrying to access direct BSP URLUse Launchpad URL (/ui#SemanticObject-action)
Login prompt (username/password) in Work ZoneURL uses -api endpoint, or SSO not configuredUse non-api URL; ensure both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant
"Destination https://https://..." errorDouble protocol in URLRemove the extra https:// prefix

Key Takeaways

  • crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.json is what creates the LADI during deployment.
  • i18n must use the simple string format ("i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties") for the LADI converter to work.
  • A separate IAM App of type External App is needed for the Launchpad tile.
  • The chain must be complete: LADI → IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role → User
  • In Work Zone, use semantic object + action — not URL tiles.
  • SSO works automatically when both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant.

Conclusion

The path from "app works in ADT preview" to "tile in Work Zone" requires a clear understanding of the deployment chain:
  • BAS deploys the UI and creates the LADI
  • The IAM App links the LADI to access control
  • Work Zone resolves the semantic intent
Each piece is simple on its own, but missing any single step results in confusing errors.
Once this flow is established, adding future apps follows the same pattern — making it repeatable and scalable across your organization.

Tags

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Business Application Studio, Fiori, RAP, ABAP Cloud, LADI, IAM App, Tile Configuration

entry.title: Clean Core by Design: Event Enablement Cockpit as a Core Integration Enabler in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Enterprise Event Enablement is one of the core components of the modern SAP Enterprise Integration Architecture for SAP S/4 HANA, which enables seamless exchange of business events across different platforms for Event-Driven communication. Events from SAP S/4 can be sent to SAP BTP Integration Suite Event Mesh/ AEM/ SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, where they can be consumed by any recipient based on the requirement.
To exchange events between SAP S/4 and  BTP EM, a communication connection setup is required. This connection is maintained by the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, which enables the data flow from the SAP S/4 HANA to the Business Event Consumer, and BTP services play it's role as the message broker(Integration Suite EM/ AEM).

A channel represents a single connection to a service instance of the SAP BTP, Event Mesh. For more information, see Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels.

Note: Channel configurations can be done via SAP Event Mesh, SAP Advance Event Mesh or SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, depending on your scenario and architecture, in this blog we will be going through SAP EM. For SAP ECC, you need ASAPIO for Events Configuration/ Enablement because Standard Business Events, like they are natively available in S/4 are not available in ECC.



SAP S/4 Event Producers

Non SAP S/4 Event Producers

Create a Destination through the SM59 transaction and setup the OAuth 2.0 configuration on the S/4 HANA system, both must be configured as per the service key instance of the corresponding event mesh instance.
After the channel creation is completed, you can list which events can be part of this channel, and an outbound binding needs to be setup to publish the events of an S/4 HANA system.
All administration, configuration, and monitoring steps are available in transaction SPRO under the Enterprise Event Enablement Node,

Under the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, below are the authorization objects and roles that are available,
Under the Hood of Enterprise Event Enablement Framework, the available authorization objects and roles are below,

Authorization Objects: /IWXBE/ADM, /IWXBE/USR, /IWXBE/SUP
