entry.title: Getting Started with GitHub in SAP Business Application Studio for SAP UI5 Development
entry.content:
Overview:

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) trial accounts expire every 90 days, making it essential to safeguard SAP UI5 projects using a reliable version control system. GitHub not only helps prevent project loss but also introduces the collaboration practices widely used in professional software development.

This blog covers:

  • Connecting SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with GitHub
  • Step-by-step process to push and manage SAP UI5 projects using GitHub.

The intent is to provide a simple and practical guide for SAP UI5 learners and developers to protect their projects, understand Git and GitHub fundamentals, and adopt industry-standard version control practices.

Introduction:

When I started learning SAP UI5, I was using a trial account on SAP Business Application Studio (BAS). 

Everything was going great — until I hit my first big obstacle. 

  • Every 90 days, the trial account expires. And when it does, everything is gone. All my projects. All my code. Vanished. 

My only option back then was to manually download each project as a zip file to my desktop before the account reset. Then when the new trial started, I had to upload everything back again. 

It was painful. It was repetitive. And one time, I forgot — and lost a project I had worked on for weeks. 

That's when I discovered GitHub — and it completely changed how I work.

I started learning how to:

  • Upload my SAP UI5 projects from SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to GitHub.
  • Access them anytime, from anywhere, even after my BAS trial account resets.
  • Share my projects easily with teammates and collaborate more effectively.
  • No more downloading ZIP files. No more worrying about the 90-day deadline. My code was safely stored in the cloud, always accessible, and backed by version control.

What Is GitHub and Why Do We Need It?

The easiest way to think about GitHub is this: it's like Google Docs for source code. Instead of collaborating on documents, developers collaborate on code while Git keeps track of every change.

GitHub is a cloud-based platform built on top of Git, a distributed version control system. It enables developers to store their source code in the cloud, track changes over time, and collaborate with others while maintaining a complete history of every modification.

In addition to version control, GitHub provides features such as repositories, branches, pull requests, and code reviews, making it easier for individuals and teams to manage software projects efficiently.

Although GitHub is widely used for open-source development, it is equally important in enterprise environments. Development teams use it to collaborate on projects, review code, manage changes, and maintain high-quality software throughout the development lifecycle.

GitHub is not just a tool for beginners. It is an industry-standard platform used by organizations of all sizes to support real-world software development and team collaboration.

In real-world software projects, developers rarely work alone. Front-end developers, backend developers, testers, and other team members often work on the same application simultaneously. Without a version control platform like GitHub, collaboration can quickly become difficult, with developers accidentally overwriting each other's changes and having no clear way to track who changed what or revert to previous versions.

GitHub solves this challenge through features such as branching. Think of the main project as a tree trunk, where each developer creates their own branch to work independently on a feature, enhancement, or bug fix. Once the work is complete, the changes are reviewed through a Pull Request (PR) and then merged back into the main branch.

This approach enables multiple developers to work in parallel without interfering with one another, maintains a complete history of code changes, and ensures that every update is reviewed before becoming part of the main project. As a result, GitHub has become an industry-standard platform for version control and team collaboration in professional software development.

A Typical Team Workflow
A typical GitHub workflow in a development team looks like this:

 
main branch          → Stable, production-ready code

├── developer-1      → Working on the Login feature
├── developer-2      → Working on the Dashboard feature
└── developer-3      → Fixing a bug in the Search feature

Each developer works on their own branch without affecting the work of others. Once a feature or bug fix is complete, the developer creates a Pull Request (PR) to request that their changes be reviewed before they are merged into the main branch.

A senior developer or technical lead reviews the code, provides feedback if any changes are required, and approves the Pull Request once the code meets the project's quality standards. After approval, the changes are merged into the main branch.

This workflow helps ensure:

  • Developers can work independently without interfering with one another.
  • Every code change is reviewed before being merged.

A complete history of changes is maintained, making it easy to track who changed what and when.

The main branch remains stable and ready for deployment.

 Now, let's walk through the step-by-step process of connecting SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to GitHub and uploading your SAP UI5 project.

Step- 1: Create a GitHub Account

  • Sign up for a GitHub account if you don't already have one.

Step 2: Initialize the Git Repository

  • Open your SAP UI5 project in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS).
  • Open the Terminal by navigating to Terminal → New Terminal.

    Run the following command to initialize a Git repository with main as the default branch:
git init -b main​

This command initializes a new Git repository in your project directory and creates the default branch named main. Git will now start tracking changes made to your project.

Step 3: Stage All Project Files

  • After initializing the Git repository, stage all the project files by running the following command in the terminal:
git add .​
  • The git add . command stages all the files and folders in the current project directory. Staging tells Git which changes should be included in the next commit.
  • Once the files are staged, Git is ready to create a snapshot of your project using a commit.
  • Note: The . (dot) represents the current directory, so git add . stages all files within your project.

Step 4: Commit the Changes

  • Once all the project files have been staged, create your first commit by running the following command:
git commit -m "Vendor Details"​
  • Creates a commit with the message "Vendor Details", which serves as a snapshot of your project at its current state.
  • Verify the Commit
  • To confirm that the commit was created successfully, run the following command:
  • git log
  • Displays the commit history of the repository, including the commit ID, author, date, and commit message.
  • If the commit was successful, you should see an output similar to:
commit 3f2c9b8e6d4f1a2b...
Author: <Your Name> <your-email@example.com>
Date:   Sat Jun 6 10:30:15 2026

    Vendor Details​
  • This confirms that your project has been committed successfully and Git is now tracking this version of your code.

Step 5: Configure Git (First-Time Setup)

  • If you're using Git for the first time, configure your username and email address using the following commands:
git config --global user.name "<github_username>"
git config --global user.email "<github_email>"
  • Configures your Git username and email address. These details are associated with every commit you create.

Step 6: Create a GitHub Repository

  • Sign in to your GitHub account.
  • Click New Repository.
  • Enter a repository name (for example, Vendor_Details_App).
  • Click Create repository.

Step 7: Connect the Local Repository to GitHub

  • After creating the repository, copy its HTTPS URL and run the following command in the BAS terminal:
git remote add origin https://github.com/sap-honey/Vendor_Details_App.git
  • Connects your local Git repository to the remote GitHub repository.

Step 8: Push the Project to GitHub

  • Push your local commits to the remote GitHub repository using the following command:
git push -u origin main​
  • Uploads your local main branch to the remote GitHub repository. The -u option sets the upstream branch, so future pushes can be performed simply by running:
git push​

  • When you run the git push command for the first time, GitHub prompts you to authenticate your account.

  • Click Copy and Open GitHub.

  • This copies the verification code and opens the GitHub device activation page in your browser.
  • GitHub automatically opens the device activation page. If prompted, sign in to your GitHub account.
  • Paste the verification code and click Continue, then click Authorize to grant SAP Business Application Studio permission to access your GitHub repository.
  • After the authorization is complete, return to SAP Business Application Studio. The git push command will finish automatically.
  • Open your GitHub account and navigate to the repository you created.
  • Refresh the repository page. You should now see all your SAP UI5 project files successfully uploaded.

    This confirms that your local SAP UI5 project has been successfully pushed to GitHub. From now on, you only need to commit your changes and run:

git push​

GitHub is an essential tool for every SAP UI5 developer. By connecting SAP Business Application Studio with GitHub, you can securely store your projects, track changes, and continue your work even after a BAS trial reset.

I hope this blog helps you get started with GitHub and makes managing your SAP UI5 projects easier and more efficient. Happy coding!

entry.title: Trigger SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from Fiori Application
entry.content:
In this blog, we will learn how to trigger an SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) workflow directly from a SAPUI5/Fiori application.

This approach is useful when users need to start approval processes, requests, or business workflows directly from the application UI.

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure the following things are done:

  1. Cloud Identity Services must be subscribed.
  2. SAP Build Process Automation must be subscribed.
  3. SAP Build Work Zone must be subscribed.
  4. Workflow must be deployed successfully.
  5. User must have the role: “ProcessAutomationParticipant”

Note: Workflow triggering from the UI will work after deployment because BPA runtime is accessed through destinations configured in SAP BTP.

You can find the complete implementation here: https://github.com/MaheshSirsat/trigger-bpa-workflow-from-fiori/tree/main 

Step 1 – Fetch SAP Build Process Automation Credentials

We need runtime credentials from the SAP Build Process Automation instance. These credentials will be used while creating the destination.

Navigation

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit.
  2. Go to Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Locate your SAP Build Process Automation instance.

 

  1. Open the instance.
  2. Click View Credentials.

 

 

The credentials contain:

  • API endpoint URL
  • OAuth client ID
  • Client secret
  • Token URL

These details allow our Fiori application to communicate securely with the BPA runtime.

Step 2 – Create Destination

We create a destination that acts as a secure bridge between the Fiori application and the SAP Build Process Automation APIs.

Navigation

  1. Open Destinations
  2. Click Create Destination

 

 

 

Configuration

Name : sap_build_process_automation

URL : endpoints.api

Authentication : OAuth2JWTBearer

Client ID : uaa.clientid

Client Secret : uaa.clientsecret

Token Service URL : uaa.url/oauth/token

Additional Properties

 

endpoints : <copy complete endpoints object>

html5-apps-repo : <copy complete html5-apps-repo object>

saasregistryenabled : true

sap.cloud.service : com.sap.spa.processautomation

sap.cloud.service.alias : spa

The destination stores all connectivity information required to call BPA APIs. Instead of hardcoding credentials inside the application, SAP BTP securely manages them through the destination.

Step 3 – Create Fiori Application

Create a SAPUI5/Fiori application that will contain a button to start the workflow.

You can create:

  • Freestyle Application
  • List Report Application

After creation, run the application locally and ensure it opens successfully.

 

Step 4 – Configure xs-app.json

Add the following route inside xs-app.json:

{

  "source": "^/workflow/(.*)$",

  "target": "/public/workflow/rest/$1",

  "destination": "sap_build_process_automation",

  "authenticationType": "xsuaa",

  "csrfProtection": false

}

 

Whenever the application calls:

/workflow/*

the App Router automatically forwards the request to the destination configured earlier.

Step 5 – Create Workflow Trigger Button

Add a button in the UI:

<mvc:View
    controllerName="project2.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m">
    <Page title="Workflow Demo">
        <Button  text="Start WF"  press="onstartWF"/>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

 

The button allows users to start the workflow directly from the UI.

Step 6 – Implement Workflow Trigger Logic

Create the following function inside the controller:

   onstartWF: async function () {
            var data = {
                definitionId: "myworkflow", // workflow-defination-id
                context: { "name": "mahesh" } // your context
            };
            await $.ajax({
                url: this._getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL() + "/workflow-instances",
                method: "POST",
                async: false,
                contentType: "application/json",

                data: JSON.stringify(data),
                success: function (result, xhr, data) {
                    sap.m.MessageToast.show('Workflow started successfully...')
                },
                error: function (request, status, error) {
                    console.error('Error in Trigger workflow')
                }
            });
        },

 

This code sends a POST request to the BPA runtime API and creates a new workflow instance.

Understanding the Payload

{
    definitionId: "myworkflow",
    context: {        "name": "mahesh"    }
}

definitionId

definitionId: "myworkflow"

This is the workflow definition ID of the deployed workflow.

 

BPA uses this value to identify which workflow should be started.

context

context: {

    "name": "mahesh"

}

This data becomes the workflow context and can be accessed inside forms, approvals, conditions, and automation steps.

Step 7 – Generate Runtime URL

Create the helper method:

_getWorkflowRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
    var appId =this.getOwnerComponent().getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
    var appPath =appId.replaceAll(".", "/");
    var appModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(appPath);
    return appModulePath + "/workflow/v1";
}

 

Instead of hardcoding BPA URLs, the application uses the App Router route configured in xs-app.json.

 

Step 8 – Build and Deploy Application

Build the application:

Deploy it to SAP BTP.

 

The workflow runtime destination and App Router configuration are available only after deployment.

Step 9 – Create Catalog, Group, and Role in Work Zone

After deployment:

  1. Create Catalog
  2. Create Group
  3. Create Role
  4. Assign application tile
  5. Assign role to user

Step 10 – Test from Launchpad

  1. Open SAP Build Work Zone.
  2. Launch the application.
  3. Click Start WF.

Expected result:

Workflow started successfully...

 

You can verify the workflow instance in SAP Build Process Automation Monitoring.

Final Result

We have successfully triggered an SAP Build Process Automation workflow directly from a Fiori application.

✔ BPA credentials are retrieved from the BPA instance.

✔ Destination securely manages authentication and connectivity.

✔ xs-app.json routes requests through App Router.

✔ UI button initiates workflow execution.

✔ Workflow context can be passed dynamically from the application.

✔ Users can start workflows directly from SAP Build Work Zone.

entry.title: Creating a Custom Action to Export Selected Records to Excel Using Fiori Elements and CAP
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a Fiori Elements application, I came across a requirement that initially seemed straightforward but wasn't available as a standard feature. The application already had the built-in Export to Excel option, but it always exported every row displayed in the table. The business users wanted something slightly different—they wanted to select only a few records and export just those.

My first thought was to implement a backend action that generated an Excel file and returned it to the UI. After exploring the available options, I realized that wasn't necessary. SAPUI5 already provides a Spreadsheet API that can generate Excel files directly in the browser, making the implementation much simpler while avoiding additional backend development.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to add a custom Export Selected button to a Fiori Elements List Report. By the end, you'll have a working example that exports only the selected rows and integrates naturally with the standard Fiori toolbar.

Step 1: Initialize the CAP Project

First, create a new directory for your project and initialize a standard SAP CAP application. Open your terminal and run:

mkdir employee
cd employee
cds init

Step 2: Define the Database Schema

Next, we will define the data model for our employees.

Create a file named schema.cds inside the db folder and add the following code:

// db/schema.cds
namespace company.hr;
 
entity Employees {
    key ID     : UUID;
    name       : String;
    department : String;
    role       : String;
    email      : String;
}

Step 3: Create the Service and UI Annotations

Now, expose the database entity as an OData service and add the basic Fiori UI annotations to define what columns will appear in our Fiori List Report table.

Create a file named cat-services.cds (typically in the srv folder) and add this code:

// srv/cat-services.cds
using { company.hr as db } from '../db/schema';
 
service EmployeeService {
    entity Employees as projection on db.Employees;
}
 
annotate EmployeeService.Employees with @(
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Employee Name', Value : name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Department', Value : department },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Role', Value : role },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Email', Value : email }
    ]
);

Step 4: Add Mock Data

To test our application, we need some dummy data. SAP CAP automatically loads CSV files that match the namespace and entity.

Create a folder named data inside your db folder, then create a file named company.hr-Employees.csv and paste the following:

ID;name;department;role;email
1;Alice Walker;Engineering;Backend Developer;alice@company.com
2;Bob Smith;Sales;Account Executive;bob@company.com
3;Charlie Davis;Engineering;Frontend Developer;charlie@company.com
4;Diana Prince;HR;HR Manager;diana@company.com

Step 5: Configure the Custom Action in the Page Map

Once your Fiori Elements application has been generated, the next step is to add a custom action to the table toolbar.

Follow these steps:

  1. Right-click on the webapp folder in your project.
  2. Select Show Page Map.
  3. In the Page Map, select the List Report page.
  4. Click the Edit (pencil) icon to modify the page.
  5. Under the Table / Table Toolbar section, click the + button next to Actions.
  6. Select Add Custom Action.

Configure the custom action with the following properties:

  • Label: Export Selected
  • Requires Selection: Enabled
  • Press Event: exportSelected

Step 6: Implement the Custom UI5 Export Logic

Now, we will write the logic that extracts the selected rows and converts them to an Excel file using the Fiori V4 API.

Open the newly generated controller extension file (ListReportExt.js) and replace its contents with the following:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Spreadsheet, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";
 
    return {
        exportSelected: function (oEvent) {
            
            // 1. Access the Fiori V4 API
            var oExtensionAPI = this.editFlow ? this : this.getExtensionAPI();
            
            // 2. Get only the rows selected by the user
            var aSelection = oExtensionAPI.getSelectedContexts();
 
            if (!aSelection || aSelection.length === 0) {
                MessageToast.show("Please select at least one employee to export.");
                return;
            }
 
            // 3. Extract the raw JSON data from those selected rows
            var aData = aSelection.map(function (oContext) {
                return oContext.getObject();
            });
 
            // 4. Map the Excel columns to your CDS database fields
            var aColumns = [
                { label: 'Employee Name', property: 'name' },
                { label: 'Department', property: 'department' },
                { label: 'Role', property: 'role' },
                { label: 'Email', property: 'email' }
            ];
 
            // 5. Configure the Excel Workbook properties
            var oSettings = {
                workbook: { 
                    columns: aColumns,
                    context: {
                        sheetName: 'Selected Employees'
                    }
                },
                dataSource: aData,
                fileName: "Custom_Employee_Export.xlsx",
                worker: false 
            };
 
            // 6. Build and Download the Excel file
            var oSheet = new Spreadsheet(oSettings);
            
            oSheet.build().then(function() {
                MessageToast.show("Excel export successful!");
            }).catch(function(sMessage) {
                MessageToast.show("Export failed: " + sMessage);
            }).finally(function () {
                oSheet.destroy(); // Destroy object to prevent memory leaks in the browser
            });
        }
    };
});

Step 7: Run and Test the Application

With the code in place, start your server by running:

cds watch

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After running the application, select one or more employee records from the table. You'll notice that the Export Selected button is enabled only when records are selected. Clicking the button downloads an Excel file containing only the selected employees instead of the entire table.

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

What I liked most about this approach is that it keeps the implementation lightweight. Since the Excel file is generated in the browser using the SAPUI5 Spreadsheet library, there was no need to create a custom CAP action or write backend logic just for exporting data.

One thing I found useful was enabling Requires Selection for the custom action. Without it, users could click the button even when no rows were selected, resulting in unnecessary validation. Letting Fiori handle the button state provides a much better user experience.

Although this example exports employee data, the same approach can be used in almost any Fiori Elements application, such as Sales Orders, Purchase Orders, Expense Reports, or Products. If you've ever wanted to provide users with more control over what they export, this is a simple customization that's worth adding to your applications.
